text: Philosophers have long debated how much of the world we consciously see at once. Cognitive science has quietly answered: surprisingly little. Change blindness experiments, pioneered by Ronald Rensink, show that large alterations to a scene go undetected when a brief disruption — a flicker, a cut, a blink — intervenes between views. Observers confidently report seeing the scene in full, but their change-detection failures reveal the opposite. The brain constructs an impression of a rich, complete visual world while holding far less in active representation than it seems. The experience of seeing is partly confabulation, a continuous plausible story told about a sparse sample.
question: What do Ronald Rensink's change blindness experiments reveal about visual perception?

options.text: The brain holds a complete and accurate representation of every visual scene
Large visual changes are easily noticed when attention is directed to the scene
The brain actively constructs a rich impression from a much sparser actual representation
Change detection failures occur only when the disruption lasts longer than one second
